subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 14! In earlier units you learned to multiply and divide whole numbers with confidence. Now you will take a big and exciting step forward: you will learn to multiply and divide with fractions and with decimals. These are the numbers that describe the parts of things – half a cup of flour, three quarters of a kilometre, 2.5 litres of camel milk, or the 7.20 shillings change you get at the market. Being able to calculate with them turns you into someone who can solve real, everyday problems.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the standardized Cambridge Stage 6 workbook source rows for screen presentation.

outcomes: Multiply a proper fraction by a whole number and a fraction by a fraction, then simplify the result.
Convert between improper fractions and mixed numbers as part of a calculation.
Divide a fraction by a whole number and a fraction by a fraction using the reciprocal.
Multiply and divide with mixed numbers by first converting them to improper fractions.
Multiply decimals by whole numbers, by two-digit numbers, and by other decimals, placing the decimal point correctly.
Divide decimals by whole numbers and by other decimals using long division and the equal-move method.
Convert between fractions and decimals and solve problems that mix the two.
Estimate answers first and check results using inverse operations and reasonableness.

concepts.title: Multiplying a Fraction by a Whole Number
concepts.explanation: Multiplying a fraction by a whole number simply means taking that fraction several times over. Suppose each guest at Farah's iftar receives 1/4 of a large anjero. If three guests each take 1/4, then together they have taken 1/4 + 1/4 + 1/4, which is 3/4. Adding the same fraction three times is exactly what 3 × 1/4 means, so 3 × 1/4 = 3/4. This gives us a clear rule. To multiply a fraction by a whole number, multiply the numerator by the whole number and keep the denominator the same, then simplify if you can. The…
concepts.example: Picture a chocolate bar split into 5 equal pieces, so each piece is 1/5 of the bar. Taking 2 pieces gives 2/5. Now suppose you want 3 × 2/5, meaning three groups of 2/5. You could imagine three bars, taking 2 pieces…

concepts.title: Multiplying a Fraction by a Fraction
concepts.explanation: Sometimes you need a fraction of a fraction. When Maryam eats 1/3 of a plate of rice that was itself 3/4 of a full plate, she has eaten 1/3 of 3/4. In mathematics the word of means multiply, so she has eaten 1/3 × 3/4 of a full plate. The rule for multiplying two fractions is beautifully simple: multiply the numerators together, and multiply the denominators together, then simplify. So 1/3 × 3/4 = (1 × 3)/(3 × 4) = 3/12, and 3/12 simplifies to 1/4. Maryam ate one quarter of a full plate of rice. Notice that…
concepts.example: Let us do another. Calculate 2/3 × 3/4. Multiply numerators: 2 × 3 = 6. Multiply denominators: 3 × 4 = 12. That gives 6/12, which simplifies to 1/2. A neat shortcut is to cancel before you multiply: the 3 on top and the…

concepts.title: Dividing a Fraction by a Whole Number
concepts.explanation: Dividing a fraction by a whole number means sharing that fraction into equal parts. Imagine Hassan has 3/4 of a cake and wants to share it equally among 3 people. He must work out 3/4 ÷ 3. He has 3 quarter-pieces, and he shares them among 3 people, so each person receives 1 quarter-piece, which is 1/4 of the whole cake. Sharing 3/4 among 3 gives 1/4. There is a simple rule that always works. To divide a fraction by a whole number, keep the numerator the same and multiply the denominator by the whole number, then…
concepts.example: Worked example: calculate 2/3 ÷ 4. Keep the numerator, 2. Multiply the denominator by 4: 3 × 4 = 12. So the answer is 2/12, which simplifies to 1/6. You can also picture this with the reciprocal idea from the next…

concepts.title: Dividing by a Fraction – Using the Reciprocal
concepts.explanation: Dividing by a fraction can seem strange at first, because the answer is often bigger than the number you started with. Think about it with a real question: how many 1/4-cup scoops can you fill from 2 cups of bariis (rice)? Each cup holds four quarter-cups, so 2 cups hold 8 quarter-cups. That means 2 ÷ 1/4 = 8. Dividing by a small fraction gives a large answer, because many small pieces fit inside the whole. The rule that makes this easy is: to divide by a fraction, multiply by its reciprocal. The reciprocal of a…
concepts.example: Worked example: calculate 2/3 ÷ 1/6. The reciprocal of 1/6 is 6/1. So 2/3 ÷ 1/6 = 2/3 × 6/1 = 12/3 = 4. Another: calculate 5/8 ÷ 2/3. The reciprocal of 2/3 is 3/2, so 5/8 ÷ 2/3 = 5/8 × 3/2 = 15/16. Because 15 and 16…

concepts.title: Working with Mixed Numbers
concepts.explanation: A mixed number such as 1 1/2 has a whole part and a fraction part. Before you can multiply or divide with a mixed number, you must first change it into an improper fraction. To do this, multiply the whole number by the denominator, add the numerator, and place the result over the same denominator. For 1 1/2: multiply 1 × 2 = 2, add the numerator 1 to get 3, and place it over 2, giving 3/2. Worked example (multiplying): calculate 2 1/2 × 1 1/5. Convert both: 2 1/2 = 5/2 and 1 1/5 = 6/5. Now multiply: 5/2 × 6/5 =…
concepts.example: Worked example (dividing): calculate 2 2/3 ÷ 2/3. Convert the mixed number: 2 2/3 = 8/3. Now divide by multiplying by the reciprocal: 8/3 ÷ 2/3 = 8/3 × 3/2 = 24/6 = 4. This answers a real question – a plank 2 2/3 metres…

concepts.title: Multiplying Decimals by Whole Numbers
concepts.explanation: Decimals are another way of writing parts of a whole, and they appear everywhere in money and measurement. To multiply a decimal by a whole number, you use the same method as ordinary multiplication, with one extra step to place the decimal point. The key idea rests on place value: the first digit after the point is tenths, the second is hundredths, the third is thousandths. Here is the method. First, ignore the decimal point and multiply as if both numbers were whole numbers. Then count the decimal places in the…
concepts.example: Always estimate first to check you are sensible. For 3.45 × 6, round 3.45 to 3, giving about 3 × 6 = 18. Our answer 20.7 is close to 18, so it is reasonable. If we had accidentally written 207 or 2.07, the estimate…

methods.title: How to multiply a fraction by a fraction
steps: Convert any mixed numbers to improper fractions.
Multiply the numerators together.
Multiply the denominators together.
Simplify the result to lowest terms.

methods.title: How to divide by a fraction
steps: Convert any mixed numbers to improper fractions.
Keep the first fraction as it is.
Flip the second fraction to make its reciprocal.
Multiply, then simplify.

methods.title: How to multiply and divide decimals
steps: Estimate the answer using rounded numbers.
For multiplying: multiply as whole numbers, then count the decimal places.
For dividing by a decimal: move both points to make the divisor whole.
Check against your estimate, and multiply back to confirm a division.

methods.title: Dividing by a fraction
steps: Solution: Keep the first fraction, flip the second, and multiply: 3/4 × 8/3 = 24/12 = 2.
So 3/8 fits into 3/4 exactly twice, which makes sense because 3/8 is half of 3/4.
Check the dividing by a fraction result carefully.

methods.title: Mixed numbers
steps: Solution: Convert the mixed number: 1 1/2 = 3/2.
Now multiply: 3/2 × 2/3 = 6/6 = 1.
Converting to an improper fraction first turned a tricky-looking problem into a simple one.

methods.title: Decimal times a whole number
steps: Solution: Ignore the point: 75 × 6 = 450.
The number 0.75 has 2 decimal places, so the answer needs 2 places: 4.50 = 4.5.
Estimate check: about 1 × 6 = 6, and 0.75 is three quarters of that, giving roughly 4.5.
Reasonable.

workedExamples.title: Fraction times a whole number
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Multiply the numerator by the whole number: 4 × 3 = 12. Keep the denominator: 5. That gives 12/5, an improper fraction. Convert to a mixed number: 5 goes into 12 twice with 2 left over, so the answer is 2 2/5.

workedExamples.title: Fraction times a fraction
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Multiply the tops: 4 × 3 = 12. Multiply the bottoms: 9 × 8 = 72. That gives 12/72. Simplify by dividing both by 12: 1/6. (You could also cancel first – the 3 and the 9 share a factor of 3, and the 4 and 8 share a factor of 4 – reaching 1/6 more quickly.)

workedExamples.title: Fraction divided by a whole number
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Keep the numerator, 3; multiply the denominator by 3: 4 × 3 = 12. That gives 3/12, which simplifies to 1/4. Check with the reciprocal method: 3/4 × 1/3 = 3/12 = 1/4. The two methods agree.

workedExamples.title: Dividing by a fraction
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Keep the first fraction, flip the second, and multiply: 3/4 × 8/3 = 24/12 = 2. So 3/8 fits into 3/4 exactly twice, which makes sense because 3/8 is half of 3/4.

workedExamples.title: Mixed numbers
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Convert the mixed number: 1 1/2 = 3/2. Now multiply: 3/2 × 2/3 = 6/6 = 1. Converting to an improper fraction first turned a tricky-looking problem into a simple one.

workedExamples.title: Decimal times a whole number
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Ignore the point: 75 × 6 = 450. The number 0.75 has 2 decimal places, so the answer needs 2 places: 4.50 = 4.5. Estimate check: about 1 × 6 = 6, and 0.75 is three quarters of that, giving roughly 4.5. Reasonable.

workedExamples.title: Decimal times a decimal
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Ignore the points: 14 × 23 = 322. Total decimal places: 1 + 1 = 2, so the answer is 3.22. Estimate check: about 1 × 2 = 2 up to 2 × 3 = 6, and 3.22 sits sensibly in that range.

workedExamples.title: Dividing a decimal by a whole number
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Place the point in the answer above the point in 12.6. Divide: 12 ÷ 3 = 4, then bring down the 6 (after the point) and 6 ÷ 3 = 2, giving 4.2. Check: 4.2 × 3 = 12.6.

workedExamples.title: Dividing by a decimal
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Move both decimal points one place to the right to make the divisor whole: 72 ÷ 18 = 4. Check: 4 × 1.8 = 7.2.

workedExamples.title: A mixed-form problem
workedExamples.prompt: A tank holds 60 litres when full and is currently 5/6 full. How many more litres are needed to fill it?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The tank holds 5/6 × 60 = 50 litres now. To fill it needs 60 − 50 = 10 litres. Here a fraction-of-a-whole calculation and a subtraction work together to answer one real question.

activities.title: Recipe Scaling Chef
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and a favourite family recipe (or the one below).
steps: Base recipe (makes 12 anjero): 1/2 cup flour, 1/3 cup water starter, 3/4 cup mixed flour, 1/8 teaspoon salt.
Double the recipe to make 24 anjero: multiply every ingredient by 2 (for example flour: 1/2 × 2 = 1 cup).
Make a half batch of 6: divide every ingredient by 2 (for example flour: 1/2 ÷ 2 = 1/4 cup).
Make 18 anjero by multiplying every ingredient by 1 1/2 (that is 3/2).
Write your own recipe and scale it up and down. Check that your scaled amounts still make sense.

activities.title: Market Decimal Shopping
activities.materials: paper, pencil, and this pretend price list (shillings): mango 2.45, banana 0.80, sambusa 1.25, camel milk 3.50 per litre.
steps: Work out the cost of 3.5 kilograms of mango at 2.45 per kilogram.
Work out the cost of 6 sambusas, then of 2.5 litres of camel milk.
You have 20 shillings. Choose items to buy, add up the cost, and find your change.
Split the total bill equally among 4 people by dividing – how much does each person pay?
This activity practises decimal multiplication (costs) and decimal division (splitting the bill), exactly as families do at the market.

activities.title: Rope and Ribbon Cutting Investigation
activities.materials: a length of string or ribbon, a ruler, and scissors (ask an adult first).
steps: Cut a piece exactly 2.4 metres long (or use 24 centimetres to stand for 2.4 metres).
Predict how many 0.3 metre pieces you can cut, using 2.4 ÷ 0.3.
Now cut the pieces and count them. Did your calculation match?
Try again with a 3/4 metre rope cut into 1/8 metre pieces (3/4 ÷ 1/8). Predict, then check.
Seeing the pieces with your own eyes shows why dividing by a small amount gives a large number of pieces.

activities.title: Fraction and Decimal Matching Game
steps: Make cards showing fractions (1/2, 1/4, 3/4, 1/5, 3/5, 3/8) and matching decimal cards (0.5, 0.25, 0.75, 0.2, 0.6, 0.375).
Shuffle all the cards and lay them face down in a grid.
Take turns flipping two cards, trying to match a fraction with its equal decimal.
If they match, keep the pair and go again. The player with the most pairs wins.
This game builds the quick fraction-decimal conversions that make mixed problems much easier.

activities.title: Sports Statistics Analyst (Family Task)
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and a family member to work with.
steps: Imagine a football player scored 156 goals over 12 seasons. Find the average per season: 156 ÷ 12.
Make up three more players with total goals and seasons, and find each average.
Rank the players from highest to lowest average per season.
Predict a player's total for a 20-season career by multiplying their average by 20.
Averages are decimal division in action – the same maths sports commentators use every week.

activities.title: Detective Investigation – The Shared Bill
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and a detective mindset.
steps: The case: Five friends order 3 pizzas at 18.75 shillings each and 2 bottles of juice at 3.25 shillings each, then add a 6.50 tip.
Find the pizza cost: 3 × 18.75.
Find the juice cost: 2 × 3.25.
Add the tip and find the total bill.
Divide the total equally among 5 friends to find each person's share. Show every step of your solution.

rules.title: Key rule 1
rules.text: Multiplying Fractions Multiply the numerators, multiply the denominators, then simplify. Fraction times a whole number: multiply the numerator only, keep the denominator. The word 'of' means multiply.

rules.title: Key rule 2
rules.text: Dividing Fractions Keep the first fraction, flip the second (its reciprocal), then multiply. Fraction divided by a whole number: keep the numerator, multiply the denominator. Keep, flip, multiply.

rules.title: Key rule 3
rules.text: Multiplying Decimals Multiply as whole numbers, ignoring the point. The answer has as many decimal places as the total in both factors. Estimate first to place the point correctly.

rules.title: Key rule 4
rules.text: Dividing Decimals By a whole number: put the point in the answer above the point in the dividend, then divide. By a decimal: move both points right until the divisor is whole, then divide.

terms: The top / bottom number of a fraction
A whole number and a fraction (e.g. 1 3/4)
A fraction flipped upside down (2/3 becomes 3/2)
Write a fraction in lowest terms
A position after the point: tenths, hundredths, thousandths
A quick, sensible answer used to check the exact one
commonMistakes: Keep the top, multiply the bottom: 2/12 = 1/6
Move the point in both numbers equally

To multiply a decimal by a whole number, you use the same method as ordinary multiplication, with one extra step to place the decimal point. The key idea rests on place value: the first digit after the point is tenths, the second is hundredths, the third is thousandths. Here is the method. questions.question: What does “Numerator / Denominator” mean?
options: The top / bottom number of a fraction
A whole number and a fraction (e.g. 1 3/4)
A fraction flipped upside down (2/3 becomes 3/2)
questions.answer: The top / bottom number of a fraction
questions.hint: Use the Unit 14 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Numerator / Denominator means The top / bottom number of a fraction.

questions.question: What does “Improper fraction” mean?
options: A whole number and a fraction (e.g. 1 3/4)
A fraction flipped upside down (2/3 becomes 3/2)
Write a fraction in lowest terms
questions.hint: Use the Unit 14 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Improper fraction means Numerator larger than denominator (e.g. 7/4).

questions.question: What does “Mixed number” mean?
options: A whole number and a fraction (e.g. 1 3/4)
A fraction flipped upside down (2/3 becomes 3/2)
Write a fraction in lowest terms
A position after the point: tenths, hundredths, thousandths
questions.answer: A whole number and a fraction (e.g. 1 3/4)
questions.hint: Use the Unit 14 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Mixed number means A whole number and a fraction (e.g. 1 3/4).

questions.question: What does “Reciprocal” mean?
options: A fraction flipped upside down (2/3 becomes 3/2)
Write a fraction in lowest terms
A position after the point: tenths, hundredths, thousandths
A quick, sensible answer used to check the exact one
questions.answer: A fraction flipped upside down (2/3 becomes 3/2)
questions.hint: Use the Unit 14 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Reciprocal means A fraction flipped upside down (2/3 becomes 3/2).

selfAssessment: I can multiply a proper fraction by a whole number and a fraction by a fraction, then simplify the result.
I can convert between improper fractions and mixed numbers as part of a calculation.
I can divide a fraction by a whole number and a fraction by a fraction using the reciprocal.
I can multiply and divide with mixed numbers by first converting them to improper fractions.
I can multiply decimals by whole numbers, by two-digit numbers, and by other decimals, placing the decimal point correctly.
I can divide decimals by whole numbers and by other decimals using long division and the equal-move method.
I can convert between fractions and decimals and solve problems that mix the two.
I can estimate answers first and check results using inverse operations and reasonableness.
